icd_category: J91.8 — Pleural effusion in other conditions classified elsewhere
one_liner: A 72-year-old woman with a history of congestive heart failure presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion and bilateral lower extremity edema over 2 weeks.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports gradually worsening shortness of breath over 2 weeks, now occurring with minimal exertion (walking to the bathroom). She sleeps propped up on 3 pillows (baseline is 1) and has had two episodes of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. She notes increasing bilateral ankle swelling and a 4 kg weight gain in 2 weeks. She admits to dietary indiscretion (high salt meals) and missed her furosemide doses for 5 days. She denies chest pain, fever, cough, or hemoptysis.
stage2.pmh: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 30%), hypertension, atrial fibrillation, type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease stage 3.
stage2.surgical_history: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) x3, 5 years ago.
stage2.medications: Furosemide 40 mg daily (non-compliant), lisinopril 20 mg daily, carvedilol 12.5 mg BID, spironolactone 25 mg daily, apixaban 5 mg BID, metformin 500 mg BID, atorvastatin 40 mg daily.
stage2.family_history: Father died of MI at age 65. Mother had hypertension and diabetes.
stage2.social_history: Retired teacher. Never smoker. No alcohol. Lives alone. Has a home health aide 3 days/week.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Obese elderly woman, mildly dyspneic at rest, speaking in full sentences. HEENT: **JVP elevated to 12 cm H2O**. Lungs: **Decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion at bilateral bases (right > left)**; **fine bibasilar crackles**. Cardiovascular: **Irregularly irregular rhythm, S3 gallop present, holosystolic murmur at apex (mitral regurgitation)**. Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended, positive hepatojugular reflux, no tenderness. Extremities: **2+ bilateral pitting edema to mid-shin**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated; consistent with decompensated heart failure as cause of effusion
Mild hyponatremia — dilutional, common in decompensated CHF and associated with worse prognosis
Upper normal; monitor given renal dysfunction and spironolactone use
Elevated above baseline (1.2) — cardiorenal syndrome from poor forward flow
Elevated BUN:Cr ratio suggestive of prerenal azotemia from poor cardiac output
Mild anemia — may contribute to symptoms and exacerbate heart failure
Low-normal; contributes to reduced oncotic pressure and fluid accumulation
Pleural fluid protein/serum protein ratio <0.5 — transudative by Light criteria
Pleural fluid LDH/serum LDH ratio <0.6 — transudative by Light criteria
Normal serum LDH for Light criteria calculation
Mildly elevated transaminases from hepatic congestion secondary to right heart failure

imaging_results.result: Bilateral this conditions (right > left) with meniscus sign. Bilateral perihilar hazy opacities (pulmonary edema). Cardiomegaly with cardiothoracic ratio >0.5. Cephalization of pulmonary vasculature.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Classic findings of decompensated heart failure with bilateral transudative this conditions

imaging_results.result: Bilateral anechoic this conditions, larger on the right (~800 mL estimated). No septations or loculations. IVC dilated >2.1 cm with <50% inspiratory collapse.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Confirms bilateral simple effusions and elevated right atrial pressure consistent with volume overload

acceptable_tests: Pleural fluid cell count and differential
low_yield_tests: Pleural fluid cytology (in clearly transudative effusion)

stage5.final_clue: IV furosemide produces 3.5 L urine output over 12 hours with significant symptom improvement and interval decrease in effusion on repeat imaging.

debrief.classic_presentation: Elderly patient with known CHF presents with progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, PND, peripheral edema, and weight gain, with bilateral pleural effusions (right > left) on imaging and transudative fluid on thoracentesis.
debrief.pathophysiology: In decompensated heart failure, elevated left-sided filling pressures increase pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure, driving fluid into the interstitium and pleural space. Bilateral effusions (typically right > left due to greater right pleural surface area and lymphatic drainage patterns) are characteristic. The fluid is transudative because it results from hydrostatic pressure imbalances rather than pleural inflammation. Concurrent right heart failure raises systemic venous pressure, contributing to peripheral edema and hepatic congestion.
debrief.treatment_overview: Primary treatment: aggressive diuresis with IV loop diuretics (furosemide, typically starting at 1-2x the home oral dose given IV). Sodium and fluid restriction. Optimize neurohormonal blockade (ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker, MRA). Therapeutic thoracentesis for large symptomatic effusions (limit to 1-1.5 L per session to avoid re-expansion pulmonary edema). Address precipitants: dietary non-compliance, medication non-adherence, arrhythmia, infection. Monitor daily weights, strict I/Os, and renal function during diuresis.
high_yield_pearls: Light criteria classify effusions as exudative if ANY of the following are met: pleural/serum protein ratio >0.5, pleural/serum LDH ratio >0.6, or pleural LDH >2/3 the upper limit of normal serum LDH.
CHF effusions can be misclassified as exudative after diuresis (pseudo-exudate) — use the serum-effusion albumin gradient (>1.2 g/dL = transudate) to clarify.
Bilateral effusions with cardiomegaly in a patient with known CHF do not require diagnostic thoracentesis unless atypical features are present (unilateral, fever, pleuritic pain).
In CHF-related effusions, right-sided effusions are more common than left-sided — an isolated left-sided effusion should raise suspicion for other etiologies (e.g., PE, malignancy, TB).
Hyponatremia in decompensated CHF is a poor prognostic sign and is dilutional — fluid restriction, not sodium replacement, is the treatment.
sources: Light RW. Pleural effusions. Med Clin North Am. 2011
UpToDate: Pleural effusion in heart failure
